title: Rain Census
desc: Someone in Everrain has to measure the water that keeps this impossible cavern alive.

steps.text:
In Everrain, the shift begins with counting water. That sounds simple until you stand under the cavern roof and watch what is actually happening. Lake Ken-I-Po seeps through the ceiling in threads, veils, steady drops, and sudden silver sheets. Moss shines on the walls. Algae paints the rock green. Fungal lamps still glow in the darker corners, though the pale seep-light from above is already making them look sleepy.

A rain keeper meets you at the main ledge with a rack of crystal cups, a measuring slate, and boots slick with old spray. "Every part of this place lives on a slightly different rain," she says. "If one line weakens, another floods, and then somebody spends the day moving bridges or cursing a tunnel." She does not say this resentfully. In Everrain, weather is simply everyone's business.

The morning census splits in two. The upper ledge needs cups set beneath the major drips, or the lower pools need their moss markers and gauge rods checked before the day's traffic begins.

options.label: Carry the cup rack along the upper ledge
Check the moss markers along the lower pools

steps.text:
The upper ledge hugs the wall just beneath a curtain of cold falling water. You move from notch to notch, hanging crystal cups where the keeper points: one beneath a fast bright thread from the ceiling crack, one beneath a broad drip that hits the stone like a metronome, one beneath a misting seam that seems to water the air more than the floor.

From up here, Everrain looks less like a cave and more like a piece of weather that forgot to end. Fern beds glow dark green around the marshy edges. Fungus light pools blue in side hollows. Below, workers on the bridges are already little moving specks under the rain. Every cup you place begins to gather a different note of water - clear, mineral, cold, metallic, sweet.

The keeper checks the first filled cup against the light and smiles with obvious satisfaction. "Good spread today," she says. "The cave feels balanced." It is a practical statement, but looking out over dripping stone and green life in the middle of the UnderWorld, it also sounds very close to gratitude.

options.label: Finish the count and admire the cavern

steps.text:
The lower pools tell the story the ceiling starts. Moss bands along the stone show how high the water stood in the night. Gauge rods, set into cracks near the shore, mark whether a passage will be ankle-deep, knee-deep, or impassable by noon. You move along the edges with the keeper, reading the green lines like weather marks on a shoreline.

The pool nearest the hidden tunnel mouth is especially beautiful this morning. Its surface is nearly black under the overhang, broken only by pale floating spores and the occasional ring where a drop from above strikes and vanishes. Farther in, the tunnel itself is only a darker black, a shape inside shadow leading toward the lake overhead and all the trouble that connection invites.

You mark the rods, note the moss bands, and call the levels back to the keeper. By the end of the circuit she has a whole morning mapped in chalk. Everrain remains wet, luminous, and navigable for one more day. Here, that is no small achievement.

options.label: Hand over the slate and let the day begin
